title: Multiple Sclerosis
authors: Bruce A. C. Cree, Stephen L. Hauser

items.title: Understanding the Pathophysiology and Clinical Course of Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune disorder affecting the central nervous system, characterized by chronic inflammation, demyelination, gliosis, and neuronal loss. The disease manifests in a relapsing or progressive course, with plaques developing at different times and locations within the CNS, making it disseminated in time and space.
keyPoints: MS is an autoimmune disease of the central nervous system (CNS).
Characterized by chronic inflammation, demyelination, gliosis (plaques or scarring), and neuronal loss.
Course can be relapsing or progressive.
Plaques develop at different times and locations in the CNS (disseminated in time and space).
Approximately one million individuals affected in the United States.

items.title: Advancements in MS Treatment and Prognosis
items.content: The past decade has seen significant advancements in understanding the basic mechanisms of multiple sclerosis (MS) and developing effective therapies, particularly for its relapsing form. These developments have substantially improved long-term outcomes for patients.
keyPoints: Tremendous progress in understanding MS disease mechanisms over the past decade.
Development of highly effective therapies, especially for the relapsing form of MS.
Dramatic improvement in long-term patient outcomes.

items.title: Onset and Demographics of Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: Multiple sclerosis (MS) typically begins between ages 20 and 40, with a later onset in men compared to women. Women are affected three times more often than men.
keyPoints: Typical onset age: 20-40 years
Later onset in men
Women affected ~3x more often than men

items.title: Sensory Symptoms and Pain in Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: MS can present with varied sensory symptoms such as paresthesias, hypesthesia, and unpleasant sensations. Over 50% of patients experience pain, which can occur anywhere on the body.
keyPoints: Paresthesias: tingling, prickling, pins and needles
Hypesthesia: reduced sensation, numbness
>50% of patients experience pain

items.title: Optic Neuritis in Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: Optic neuritis (ON) is a common manifestation of MS, presenting with symptoms like diminished visual acuity and periorbital pain. An afferent pupillary defect is usually present.
keyPoints: Symptoms: diminished visual acuity, dimness, decreased color perception
Periorbital pain aggravated by eye movement
Afferent pupillary defect usually present

items.title: Understanding Paroxysmal Symptoms in MS
items.content: Paroxysmal symptoms are brief, frequent episodes lasting 10 seconds to 2 minutes and occurring 5–40 times per day. They do not alter consciousness or EEG background activity and typically last weeks to months. These symptoms can be triggered by hyperventilation or movement.
keyPoints: Duration: 10 s to 2 min
Frequency: 5–40 episodes/day
Triggers: Hyperventilation, movement
Examples: Lhermitte's symptom, tonic seizures, dysarthria, ataxia
Mechanism: Spontaneous discharges from demyelinated plaques

items.title: Lhermitte's Symptom and Its Implications in MS
items.content: Lhermitte's symptom is an electric shock-like sensation triggered by neck movement, radiating down the back into the legs. It can persist for years but often resolves on its own.
keyPoints: Trigger: Neck flexion or movements
Radiation: Down the back to legs (rarely arms)
Duration: Self-limited; may last years
Associated Conditions: Cervical spondylosis

items.title: Heat Sensitivity and Uhthoff's Symptom in MS
items.content: MS symptoms can worsen with an increase in body temperature, such as during a hot shower or exercise. This is known as Uhthoff's symptom and results from transient conduction block.
keyPoints: Symptoms: Neurologic worsening with heat
Example: Unilateral visual blurring during hot showers
Mechanism: Transient conduction block

items.title: Understanding MS Disease Course and Subtypes
items.content: Multiple Sclerosis (MS) can be categorized into three main clinical forms: relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS), secondary progressive MS (SPMS), and primary progressive MS (PPMS). While historically viewed as distinct, current understanding integrates inflammation and neurodegeneration across all stages. Clinically, the classical subtype scheme remains useful for patient assessment.
keyPoints: 90% of MS cases are RRMS, characterized by discrete attacks with potential recovery.
SPMS follows RRMS, marked by progressive deterioration without acute attacks; an EDSS ≥ 4 and FSS motor score ≥ 2 support diagnosis.
PPMS accounts for ~10% of cases, begins later in life (mean age ~40), and progresses steadily without relapses.

items.title: Progression Mechanisms in MS: RAW vs. PIRA
items.content: Disability accumulation in MS results from either relapse associated worsening (RAW) or progression independent of relapsing activity (PIRA). Initially thought to be driven by RAW, it is now understood that PIRA contributes significantly across all forms of MS.
keyPoints: PIRA events can occur silently and contribute to disability in RRMS, SPMS, and PPMS.
The distinction between RRMS and SPMS may blur if any confirmed PIRA event indicates early SPMS onset.

items.title: Impact of Therapies on MS Progression to SPMS
items.content: Historically, the risk of RRMS evolving into SPMS was ~3% annually. However, with effective therapies, this rate has decreased to <1% per year.
keyPoints: Effective therapies have significantly reduced the annual evolution rate from RRMS to SPMS.

items.title: Geographic and Demographic Patterns in MS Prevalence
items.content: Multiple Sclerosis (MS) prevalence is influenced by geographic location, with higher rates observed in temperate zones compared to tropical regions. A north-south gradient exists, showing decreasing prevalence equatorially. Over the past 50 years, MS prevalence has increased globally, more so among women and nonwhite populations.
keyPoints: Highest MS prevalence in temperate zones; ~10-20-fold less in tropical regions.
North-south gradient with decreasing rates towards the equator.
Increased MS prevalence over the past 50 years due to environmental shifts, improved diagnosis, or longer lifespan.
Greater increase in women and nonwhite populations.
In the U.S., higher prevalence in whites compared to blacks; lower estimates in Hispanics and Asians.

items.title: Environmental Factors Associated with MS Risk
items.content: Several environmental factors are linked to increased MS risk, including Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection, cigarette smoking, and vitamin D deficiency. EBV exposure significantly raises MS risk, while smoking is associated with T lymphocyte activation in animal models. Vitamin D deficiency, common at higher latitudes due to insufficient UVB radiation, is also linked to MS.
keyPoints: EBV infection increases MS risk; ~5% of the population globally are EBV-negative and have a 20-fold lower MS risk.
History of infectious mononucleosis doubles MS risk further.
Higher antibody titers to EBV nuclear antigens associated with increased MS risk.
Cigarette smoking linked to MS risk via activation of pathogenic T lymphocytes in animal models.
Vitamin D deficiency associated with MS; low UVB exposure at higher latitudes contributes to deficiency.

items.title: Genetic Susceptibility to MS
items.content: Multiple Sclerosis (MS) has a significant genetic component, with familial aggregation primarily due to genetics. The strongest genetic association is with the HLA-DRB1*1501 gene, accounting for about 10% of disease risk.
keyPoints: Familial aggregation in MS is largely genetic.
HLA-DRB1*1501 accounts for ~10% of MS risk.
MS susceptibility is polygenic with many genes having small effects.

items.title: Genetic Variants and Autoimmunity
items.content: Over 230 genetic variants associated with MS have been identified, primarily affecting the immune system. These genes include those for IL-7 receptor, IL-2 receptor, and T-cell costimulatory molecule LFA-3.
keyPoints: More than 230 MS susceptibility variants identified.
Genes affect adaptive and innate immune systems.
Variants also influence other autoimmune diseases.

items.title: Genetic Influence on Disease Progression
items.content: Recent discoveries have identified genetic loci associated with MS severity, such as the region of dysferlin and ZNF638, which can accelerate progression to wheelchair dependency by approximately 7 years.
keyPoints: Loci for MS severity recently identified.
Dysferlin and ZNF638 linked to accelerated disease progression.
Associated with a ~7-year acceleration to wheelchair dependency.

items.title: Role of B Cells in MS Pathogenesis
items.content: B cells are crucial in the development of demyelinating lesions in MS, evidenced by the efficacy of B cell-based treatments. They produce oligoclonal immunoglobulins and antibodies in CSF, which are characteristic of MS.
keyPoints: Clonally restricted populations of activated, antigen-experienced, memory B cells and plasma cells are present in MS lesions.
B cells produce myelin-specific autoantibodies, including those against MOG.
Antigen-presenting cell (APC) function of B cells is likely key to their role in MS pathogenesis.

items.title: Autoimmunity Triggered by HLA-DRB1*1501 and Molecular Mimicry
items.content: MS-associated HLA proteins, particularly HLA-DRB1*1501, may trigger autoimmunity through molecular mimicry with viral, bacterial, or host antigens.
keyPoints: Fragments of self-peptides derived from HLA-DR2 proteins bind to DRB1*1501 molecules on B cells.
Memory CD4+ T cells respond to these peptides bound to DR2 molecules.
Self-peptides are cross-reactive with myelin antigens and proteins from EBV, Akkermansia muciniphila, and RASGRP2.

items.title: Microglial Activation in Progressive MS
items.content: Activated microglia are a hallmark of progressive MS pathology, found in cortical plaques and triggered by proinflammatory B and T lymphocytes or tissue injury signals.
keyPoints: Widespread microglial activation is characteristic of progressive MS.
Microglia are activated in response to proinflammatory B and T lymphocytes or toll-like receptor signaling.
Microglial transcriptional states are varied and context-dependent.

items.title: Mechanism of Conduction Block in Demyelinated Axons
items.content: In multiple sclerosis, nerve conduction block occurs when the impulse cannot traverse a demyelinated segment. This is due to hyperpolarization from exposed voltage-dependent potassium channels under the myelin sheath. Redistribution of sodium channels allows for eventual continuous propagation.
keyPoints: Saltatory conduction in myelinated axons results in faster velocities (~70 m/s) compared to unmyelinated nerves (~1 m/s).
Conduction block occurs due to hyperpolarization from exposed potassium channels under the demyelinized sheath.
Temporary conduction block can follow a demyelinating event, with sodium channel redistribution eventually allowing impulse propagation.
Variable conduction block may occur with raised body temperature or metabolic changes, explaining clinical fluctuations.

items.title: Diagnostic Criteria for Clinically Definite Multiple Sclerosis (MS)
items.content: Diagnosis of clinically definite MS requires two episodes of symptoms and signs reflecting pathology in noncontiguous CNS white matter tracts. Symptoms must last >24 hours, separated by a month or more. MRI or evoked potentials can document additional required signs.
keyPoints: Clinically definite MS diagnosis requires two symptomatic episodes and two distinct signs from noncontiguous CNS areas.
Symptoms must persist for over 24 hours with at least one-month separation between episodes.
A second sign may be documented via MRI or evoked potentials if not evident on neurologic examination.
The second clinical event can be supported by new focal white matter lesions or an enhancing lesion with a nonenhancing lesion in an asymptomatic location on MRI.
In progressive-onset cases without relapses, intrathecal IgG synthesis may support PPMS diagnosis.

items.title: MRI as a Diagnostic Tool for MS: Identifying Characteristic Lesions
items.content: MRI is crucial in diagnosing Multiple Sclerosis (MS) due to its ability to detect characteristic abnormalities in over 95% of patients. These lesions, often asymptomatic and multifocal within the brain, brainstem, and spinal cord, are particularly diagnostic when located in specific areas such as the corpus callosum or periventricular white matter.
keyPoints: Characteristic MRI abnormalities found in >95% of MS patients
Lesions typically oriented perpendicular to ventricular surface (Dawson's fingers)
Diagnostic lesions are >6 mm and located in specific areas like the corpus callosum, periventricular white matter, brainstem, cerebellum, or spinal cord

items.title: MRI's Role in Monitoring MS Progression and Activity
items.content: Serial MRI studies reveal that inflammatory disease activity in Relapsing-Remitting Multiple Sclerosis (RRMS) occurs more frequently than clinical relapses suggest, indicating silent disease progression. The total volume of T2-weighted signal abnormalities correlates with clinical disability, while brain and spinal cord atrophy measured by MRI strongly correlate with progressive MS.
keyPoints: Serial MRI shows frequent bursts of focal inflammatory activity in RRMS
Total volume of T2-weighted signal abnormality correlates with clinical disability
Brain and spinal cord atrophy on MRI correlates more strongly with measures of disability or progression

items.title: CSF Examination in MS Diagnosis
items.content: CSF analysis is crucial for diagnosing multiple sclerosis (MS), particularly when clinical diagnosis is uncertain. Key CSF findings include mononuclear cell pleocytosis, increased intrathecally synthesized IgG, and the presence of oligoclonal bands (OCBs). OCBs are detected in over 90% of MS patients.
keyPoints: Mononuclear cell pleocytosis is present in ~25% of cases, often in young RMS patients.
A CSF protein concentration >1 g/L (>100 mg/dL) or a pleocytosis >75 cells/μL should raise suspicion against MS.
OCBs are found in >90% of MS patients and may increase over time.
CSF examination is recommended for routine MS workup, especially when diagnosis is uncertain.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID | EVOKED POTENTIALS

items.title: Role of Evoked Potentials in MS Diagnosis
items.content: Evoked potentials (EPs) are valuable for diagnosing MS, especially when clinical symptoms do not fully explain the condition. EP testing is most informative when assessing clinically uninvolved pathways, such as visual EPs in patients with spinal cord syndrome.
keyPoints: Abnormalities on one or more EP modalities occur in 80–90% of MS patients.
A marked delay in latency of a specific EP component suggests demyelination.
EP abnormalities are not specific to MS but can support diagnosis when clinical symptoms are ambiguous.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID | EVOKED POTENTIALS

items.title: When to Consider Alternative Diagnoses in Suspected MS
items.content: In patients suspected of having multiple sclerosis (MS), consider alternative diagnoses when symptoms are atypical or localized, the patient is outside typical age ranges, or clinical presentations suggest other conditions. Specific tests should be conducted based on these considerations.
keyPoints: Consider alternatives if symptoms are localized to posterior fossa, craniocervical junction, or spinal cord; patient is <15 or >60 years old; course is progressive from onset; no history of visual, sensory, or bladder symptoms; or lab findings (MRI, CSE, EPs) are atypical.
Symptoms uncommon in MS (e.g., aphasia, parkinsonism, severe muscular atrophy) favor alternative diagnoses.

Consider neuromyelitis optica (NMO), myelin oligodendrocyte protein-associated disease (MOGAD), glial fibrillary acid protein (GFAP) disorders for bilateral/severe optic neuritis or transverse myelitis.
Other considerations include acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM), Sjögren's syndrome, sarcoidosis, vascular disorders, CNS lymphoma, and infections like syphilis or Lyme disease.
Tests to exclude alternatives: erythrocyte sedimentation rate, serum B12 level, antinuclear antibodies, treponemal antibody.

items.title: Understanding EDSS Scores in MS Treatment and Prognosis
items.content: The Expanded Disability Status Scale (EDSS) is crucial for assessing neurological impairment in MS. It helps differentiate between patients with mild, non-disabling disease and those with progressive forms of MS.
keyPoints: Patients with EDSS scores <3.5 walk normally and are generally not disabled.
Patients with EDSS scores >4.0 have progressive MS (SPMS or PPMS), are gait-impaired, and often occupationally disabled.

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES
items.title: Differentiating Pseudoexacerbations from True Exacerbations in MS
items.content: It is crucial to distinguish between pseudoexacerbations and true exacerbations of multiple sclerosis (MS) as this affects treatment decisions. Pseudoexacerbations, often triggered by factors like increased ambient temperature or infections, do not warrant glucocorticoid therapy.
keyPoints: Pseudoexacerbations are caused by external factors such as fever or infection and do not require glucocorticoids.
True exacerbations may be treated with intravenous methylprednisolone (500–1000 mg/d for 3–5 days) followed by oral prednisone (60–80 mg/d, tapered over 2 weeks).
Mild attacks are often left untreated.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES
items.title: Management of Severe MS Attacks with Glucocorticoids and Plasma Exchange
items.content: For moderate to severe MS exacerbations, glucocorticoid therapy is recommended. In cases where patients do not respond to glucocorticoids, plasma exchange may be beneficial.
keyPoints: Glucocorticoids provide short-term benefit in reducing attack severity and duration but have unclear long-term effects on disease progression.
Intravenous methylprednisolone (500–1000 mg/d for 3–5 days) is commonly used, with a possible oral prednisone taper.
Plasma exchange may be considered for fulminant attacks unresponsive to glucocorticoids, involving five to seven exchanges of 40–60 mL/kg every other day for 14 days.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES

items.title: Initiation of Therapy for RMS and High-Risk CIS
items.content: Therapy should be initiated in all patients diagnosed with RMS and those presenting with a first demyelinating event who are at high risk for MS, often termed clinically isolated syndrome (CIS). The recommendation is to use highly effective disease-modifying therapies as first-line options based on evidence from long-term trials.
keyPoints: Initiate therapy in all RMS patients and those with high-risk CIS.
Use the most highly effective agents as first-line treatment.
First-line options include anti-CD20 B-cell-targeting drugs (ocrelizumab, ofatumumab, ublituximab), rituximab or biosimilar, natalizumab for JCV-negative patients, S1P modulators, or fumarates.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.title: Benefits of Early and Effective Therapy in RMS
items.content: Early initiation of effective therapy can improve long-term outcomes in MS, including prolonging the time to reach disability milestones such as SPMS and requiring assistance to ambulate. Benefits are most noticeable when treatment begins early in the relapsing stage.
keyPoints: Effective therapy improves long-term MS outcomes.
Prolongs time to reach disability milestones (e.g., SPMS, needing assistance).
Most benefits seen with early treatment during the relapsing stage.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.title: Considerations for Delayed Treatment Initiation in RMS
items.content: In some patients, especially those with mild initial RRMS course and low disease activity by MRI, it may be reasonable to delay treatment initiation. These patients should be closely monitored with periodic brain MRI scans to reassess the need for therapy if subclinical disease is detected.
keyPoints: Consider delaying treatment in patients with mild RRMS and low MRI burden.
Monitor these patients closely with periodic brain MRI scans.
Reassess need for therapy if ongoing, subclinical disease is evident on MRI.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.title: First-Line Options for Active Secondary Progressive Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: For patients with active SPMS, ocrelizumab and siponimod are considered reasonable first-line options. Ocrelizumab is approved despite not being specifically studied in this subgroup, while siponimod has shown efficacy particularly in patients with recent relapses or contrast-enhancing lesions on MRI.
keyPoints: Ocrelizumab and siponimod are reasonable first-line options for active SPMS.
Ocrelizumab is approved despite not being specifically studied in SPMS.
Siponimod reduced progression risk, especially beneficial for patients with recent relapses or contrast-enhancing lesions on MRI.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.title: Ocrelizumab's Impact on Primary Progressive Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: Ocrelizumab reduces the progression of clinical disability in PPMS by 25% and improves other markers. It is the only agent convincingly shown to modify the course of PPMS, potentially extending wheelchair-independent function by more than seven years.
keyPoints: Ocrelizumab reduces clinical disability progression in PPMS by 25%.
Improves clinical and MRI markers of inflammatory and degenerative disease activity.
Extends wheelchair-independent function by >7 years on average for the average patient with PPMS.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.title: Unproven MS Therapies Lack Scientific Support
items.content: Many therapies purported to treat multiple sclerosis (MS) have not been scientifically validated. These include various dietary approaches, megadose vitamins, and alternative treatments like acupuncture or bee stings.
keyPoints: Treatments lacking scientific scrutiny: Swank diet, Paleo diet, Wahls diet, megadose vitamins, calcium orotate, bee stings, cow colostrum, hyperbaric oxygen, procarin, chelation, acupuncture, acupressure, Chinese herbal remedies, removal of mercury-amalgam tooth fillings.
Antiviral agents and antibiotics are not recommended despite plausible infection involvement (e.g., EBV, HHV-6).
CCSVI as a cause for MS has been debunked by multiple studies; vascular repair interventions lack efficacy.
High-dose biotin trial showed no benefit in progressive MS forms.
Patients should avoid costly or potentially hazardous unproven treatments lacking biological plausibility.

items.title: Avoid Costly and Hazardous Unproven Treatments for MS
items.content: Patients with multiple sclerosis (MS) should steer clear of expensive or potentially dangerous treatments that have not been scientifically validated. These include a variety of alternative therapies and interventions.
keyPoints: Examples of unproven treatments: dietary changes, megadose vitamins, calcium orotate, bee stings, cow colostrum, hyperbaric oxygen, procarin, chelation, acupuncture, acupressure, Chinese herbal remedies, removal of mercury-amalgam tooth fillings.
Antiviral and antibiotic treatments are not advised despite potential infection links (e.g., EBV, HHV-6).
CCSVI theory for MS causation has been discredited; vascular repair surgeries lack evidence of benefit.
High-dose biotin was ineffective in improving disability in progressive MS according to a double-blind trial.
Emphasis on avoiding treatments that are costly or hazardous and lack biological plausibility.

items.title: Management of Bladder Dysfunction in MS
items.content: Bladder dysfunction in MS patients is managed based on urodynamic testing due to poor symptom-pathophysiology correlation. Initial treatments include fluid restriction and voluntary voiding, progressing to medications like beta-3 adrenergic agonists (mirabegron 25–50 mg/d, vibegron 75 mg/d) over anticholinergics to avoid cognitive side effects. Botulinum toxin injections are an option when other treatments fail.
keyPoints: Urodynamic testing guides bladder dysfunction management.
Beta-3 adrenergic agonists (mirabegron 25–50 mg/d, vibegron 75 mg/d) preferred over anticholinergics due to fewer cognitive side effects.
Botulinum toxin injections can be used for detrusor hyperreflexia when medications are ineffective or cause side effects.

items.title: Treatment of Spasticity in MS
items.content: Spasticity and spasms in MS can be managed with physical therapy, exercise, stretching, and avoiding triggers. Medications include baclofen (20–120 mg/d), diazepam (2–40 mg/d), tizanidine (8–32 mg/d), dantrolene (25–400 mg/d), and cyclobenzaprine hydrochloride (10–60 mg/d). For severe cases, a baclofen pump delivering medication directly into the CSF may be used.
keyPoints: Physical therapy, exercise, and stretching are first-line treatments for spasticity.
Medications include baclofen (20–120 mg/d), diazepam (2–40 mg/d), tizanidine (8–32 mg/d), dantrolene (25–400 mg/d), cyclobenzaprine hydrochloride (10–60 mg/d).
Baclofen pump is an option for severe spasticity.

items.title: Approach to Fatigue in MS Patients
items.content: Fatigue management includes using assistive devices, home help, and addressing underlying causes like spasticity. Medication review is crucial as some can contribute to fatigue; for instance, anticholinergics should be dosed at bedtime if possible. Pharmacological options include methylphenidate (5–25 mg/d), modafinil (100–400 mg/d), or armodafinil (150–250 mg/d) for excessive daytime somnolence.
keyPoints: Assistive devices and home help can alleviate fatigue.
Review medications that may contribute to fatigue, such as anticholinergics.
Methylphenidate (5–25 mg/d), modafinil (100–400 mg/d), armodafinil (150–250 mg/d) for excessive daytime somnolence.

items.title: Impact of Disease-Modifying Therapies on MS Prognosis
items.content: Disease-modifying therapies have significantly improved the prognosis for patients with Multiple Sclerosis (MS). Historically, a large proportion of patients experienced progressive neurologic disability. However, early treatment has been shown to slow the transition from relapsing-remitting MS (RMS) to secondary-progressive MS (SPMS), and high-efficacy therapies like ocrelizumab have further improved outcomes by reducing disease progression.
keyPoints: Before disease-modifying therapies, only 20% of patients had no functional limitation after 15 years.
Between one-third and one-half of RMS patients progressed to SPMS within 15 years, requiring assistance with ambulation.
With high-efficacy therapy, nearly 80% of RMS patients experienced no disease worsening after 10 years.
In primary-progressive MS (PPMS), more than one-third had no worsening, and over 80% remained ambulatory.

items.title: Predictors of Prognosis in Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: Certain clinical features can predict the prognosis of MS. Features suggesting a more favorable outcome include optic neuritis (ON) or sensory symptoms at onset, fewer than two relapses in the first year, and minimal impairment after 5 years. Conversely, an older age at symptom onset, male gender, greater disability, and motor signs during the first year are predictors of an aggressive disease course.
keyPoints: Favorable prognosis indicators: ON or sensory symptoms at onset, fewer than two relapses in the first year, minimal impairment after 5 years.
Predictors of early aggressive course: older age at symptom onset, male gender, greater disability, motor signs in the first year.
<10% of MS patients have a benign variant and never develop neurologic disability even when untreated.

items.subtopic: PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS
items.title: Management of MS during Pregnancy: Timing and Therapy Adjustments
items.content: In pregnant MS patients, there is a decrease in attack frequency during gestation, particularly the last trimester, but an increase in attacks within 3 months postpartum. Over the entire pregnancy year (9 months plus 3 months postpartum), disease activity remains stable. Disease-modifying therapies are typically paused during pregnancy due to potential risks, especially with natalizumab and S1P drugs that may cause rebound disease upon discontinuation. Ocrelizumab is recommended before conception for its long-lasting protection through pregnancy and the immediate postpartum period.
keyPoints: Fewer MS attacks during gestation, particularly in the last trimester
Increased attacks within 3 months postpartum
Overall disease course unaffected over a full pregnancy year (9 months of pregnancy plus 3 months postpartum)
Disease-modifying therapy generally discontinued during pregnancy
Natalizumab and S1P drugs pose risk of rebound disease activity upon discontinuation
Ocrelizumab can mitigate this risk, providing long-lasting protection through pregnancy and immediate postpartum period
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS

items.subtopic: PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS
items.title: Fertility Treatments and MS: Evaluating Risks
items.content: Earlier studies suggested that fertility treatments might exacerbate MS, but more recent research indicates little to no additional risk. This evolving understanding is crucial for advising patients contemplating pregnancy about the safety of using such treatments.
keyPoints: Early concerns existed regarding fertility treatments worsening MS
Recent studies show little if any additional risk from fertility treatments
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS

items.question: Which characteristic is NOT typically associated with multiple sclerosis (MS)?
items.explanation: MS affects the central nervous system, not peripheral nerves.

items.question: What is a defining feature of MS plaques in terms of their development?
options: They develop simultaneously and at the same location.
They are disseminated in time and space.
They only occur in the brain.
They do not involve neuronal loss.
items.explanation: MS plaques typically develop at different times and in different CNS locations, indicating dissemination in time and space.

items.question: Which statement best describes the clinical course of MS?
options: It can be relapsing or progressive.
items.explanation: MS can have a relapsing or progressive course, with significant variability in its clinical presentation.

items.question: Which symptom of multiple sclerosis (MS) can present as a monocular or bilateral visual disturbance, often accompanied by periorbital pain?
items.explanation: Optic neuritis presents with visual disturbances and is often accompanied by periorbital pain.

items.question: In multiple sclerosis, what type of weakness is typically observed in the limbs?
items.explanation: Weakness in MS is usually of the upper motor neuron type, often accompanied by pyramidal signs.

items.question: Which symptom in multiple sclerosis can be mistaken for idiopathic Bell's palsy due to its presentation?
options: Facial weakness from a pontine lesion
items.explanation: Facial weakness due to a lesion in the pons can resemble idiopathic Bell's palsy.

items.question: What is a common characteristic of exercise-induced weakness in multiple sclerosis?
options: It indicates lower motor neuron involvement.
It is characterized by fatigue and loss of strength.
items.explanation: Exercise-induced weakness in MS is characterized by fatigue and loss of strength.

items.question: Which symptom is characterized by an electric shock-like sensation radiating down the back into the legs, often induced by neck flexion?
items.explanation: Lhermitte's symptom is described as an electric shock-like sensation radiating down the back into the legs, typically induced by neck flexion.

items.question: What condition can cause unilateral visual blurring during a hot shower or physical exercise?
items.explanation: Uhthoff's symptom refers to neurologic symptoms, such as unilateral visual blurring, produced by an elevation of the body's core temperature.

items.question: Which type of bladder dysfunction in MS is characterized by difficulty initiating or stopping the urinary stream due to loss of synchronization between detrusor and sphincter muscles?
items.explanation: Detrusor sphincter dyssynergia involves loss of synchronization between detrusor and sphincter muscles, causing difficulty initiating or stopping the urinary stream.

items.question: Which symptom is described as very brief lancinating facial pain often triggered by afferent input from the face or teeth?
items.explanation: Trigeminal neuralgia is characterized by very brief lancinating facial pain often triggered by afferent input from the face or teeth.

items.question: Which clinical form of MS is characterized by discrete attacks of neurologic dysfunction that generally evolve over days to weeks?
items.explanation: Relapsing-remitting MS (RRMS) is characterized by discrete attacks of neurologic dysfunction that evolve over days to weeks.

items.question: What percentage of MS cases does primary progressive MS (PPMS) account for?
items.explanation: Primary progressive MS (PPMS) accounts for ~10% of MS cases.

items.question: What is the practical definition for secondary progressive MS (SPMS)?
options: A patient with an EDSS score of 2 or greater.
A patient who experiences acute attacks without progression.
A patient with permanent walking disability not due exclusively to relapses.
A patient who does not experience any neurologic dysfunction.
items.explanation: SPMS is defined as a patient who has developed some level of permanent walking disability not due exclusively to relapses.

items.question: Which process was once thought to be the primary driver of disability accumulation in RRMS but is now considered secondary?
items.explanation: Relapse associated worsening (RAW) was once thought to be the primary driver, but PIRA is now considered the main cause of disability accumulation in RRMS.

items.question: Which geographic regions have the highest prevalence of Multiple Sclerosis (MS)?
items.explanation: The highest prevalence of MS is generally found in temperate zones.

items.question: What factor has been repeatedly associated with an increased risk of developing Multiple Sclerosis (MS)?
items.explanation: Multiple lines of evidence incriminate a role for infection with the Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) in MS.

items.question: Which demographic group has shown a greater increase in MS prevalence over the past half-century?
items.explanation: The increase in MS prevalence appears to have occurred to a greater degree in women than men and in nonwhite populations.

items.question: Which gene is associated with the strongest susceptibility signal for multiple sclerosis?
items.explanation: The HLA-DRB1*1501 gene in the class II region of the MHC is associated with the strongest susceptibility signal for MS.

items.question: What percentage of disease risk does the association with HLA-DRB1*1501 account for in multiple sclerosis?
items.explanation: The association with HLA-DRB1*1501 accounts for approximately 10% of the disease risk.

items.question: What is a characteristic of MS-associated genetic variants identified by whole-genome association studies?
options: They are highly specific and sensitive for MS diagnosis.
Each has only a very small effect on MS risk.
They have no role in the immune system.
They all operate within the nervous system.
items.explanation: The variants identified by whole-genome association studies each individually have only a very small effect on MS risk.

items.question: Which of the following genes is associated with both multiple sclerosis and other autoimmune diseases?
items.explanation: The IL7 receptor gene is associated with susceptibility to multiple sclerosis and other autoimmune diseases.

items.question: Which type of cells are centrally involved in the development of demyelinating lesions in MS?
items.explanation: B lymphocytes are centrally involved in the development of demyelinating lesions, as evidenced by the efficacy of B cell-based treatments.

items.question: In progressive MS, what characterizes inflammation?
options: Focal perivenular infiltration of lymphocytes and monocytes
Widespread microglial proliferation across large areas of white matter
Reduced myelin staining without axonal injury
items.explanation: In progressive MS, inflammation is characterized by widespread microglial proliferation across large areas of white matter.

items.question: What role do B cells play in the pathogenesis of MS?
options: Produce oligoclonal immunoglobulins in peripheral blood
items.explanation: The antigen-presenting cell (APC) function of B cells explains their role in MS pathogenesis.

items.question: Which proteins are fragments derived from HLA-DR2 that bind intact DRB1*1501 molecules on B cells?
items.explanation: Fragments of self-peptides derived from HLA-DR2 proteins bind intact DRB1*1501 molecules on B cells and serve as antigens for presentation to T cells.

items.question: Which mechanism allows for faster nerve conduction velocities in myelinated axons compared to unmyelinated nerves?
options: Continuous propagation of the nerve impulse along the entire length of the axon.
Saltatory conduction, where the nerve impulse jumps from one node of Ranvier to the next.
Increased diameter of the axonal membrane in myelinated fibers.
Higher density of sodium channels throughout the myelinated segments.
items.explanation: Saltatory conduction allows for faster nerve conduction velocities by jumping from one node of Ranvier to the next.

items.question: What can cause a temporary conduction block in demyelinated axons?
options: Hyperpolarization due to exposure of voltage-dependent potassium channels.
Redistribution of sodium channels away from the nodes.
Continuous propagation through the entire length of the axon.
items.explanation: A temporary conduction block can occur due to hyperpolarization caused by exposure of voltage-dependent potassium channels in demyelinated segments.

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as potentially affecting variable conduction block?
options: Redistribution of sodium channels to the demyelinated segment.
items.explanation: Redistribution of sodium channels is not mentioned as a factor affecting variable conduction block; it helps restore conduction after an initial block.

items.question: Which MRI finding serves as a marker of inflammation in MS?
options: Leakage of intravenous gadolinium into the parenchyma
items.explanation: Leakage of intravenous gadolinium (Gd) into the parenchyma is an early marker of inflammation in MS.

items.question: What does a 'black hole' on T1-weighted imaging indicate in MS?
items.explanation: 'Black holes' on T1-weighted imaging are markers of irreversible demyelination and axonal loss.

items.question: What is the typical duration for gadolinium enhancement in MS lesions?
items.explanation: Gadolinium enhancement typically persists for less than 1 month in MS lesions.

items.question: Which of the following findings in cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is most specific for multiple sclerosis (MS)?
options: Two or more discrete oligoclonal bands not present in serum
items.explanation: Two or more discrete oligoclonal bands not present in serum are found in >90% of patients with MS.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID | EVOKED POTENTIALS

items.question: What CSF finding should raise concern that a patient may not have multiple sclerosis (MS)?
options: Increased level of intrathecally synthesized IgG
Two or more discrete oligoclonal bands
items.explanation: A protein concentration >1 g/L in CSF should raise concern that the patient may not have MS.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID | EVOKED POTENTIALS

items.question: What is the significance of a mild CSF pleocytosis (>5 cells/μL) in multiple sclerosis (MS)?
options: It is present in ~25% of cases, usually in young patients with RMS.
It confirms the diagnosis of MS.
items.explanation: A mild CSF pleocytosis (>5 cells/μL) is present in ~25% of cases, usually in young patients with RMS.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID | EVOKED POTENTIALS

items.question: Which of the following symptoms would most likely suggest an alternative diagnosis to multiple sclerosis (MS)?
options: Mild symptoms with a normal neurological examination
Gait impairment in patients with EDSS scores >4.0
items.explanation: The absence of visual, sensory, or bladder symptoms favors an alternative diagnosis to MS.

items.question: Which disorder should be considered in patients presenting with bilateral and/or severe optic neuritis or severe transverse myelitis?
items.explanation: Neuromyelitis optica (NMO) should be considered in patients with bilateral and/or severe optic neuritis or severe transverse myelitis.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a category of therapy for multiple sclerosis (MS)?
options: Disease-modifying agents that reduce MS activity
Treatments promoting remyelination or neural repair
items.explanation: Treatments promoting remyelination or neural repair do not currently exist as a category of therapy for MS.

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES
items.question: Which of the following is an inappropriate treatment for a pseudoexacerbation in multiple sclerosis?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids are inappropriate for pseudoexacerbations as they reflect non-disease activity changes.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES
items.question: What is the typical initial intravenous dosage of methylprednisolone used to manage acute attacks in multiple sclerosis?
items.explanation: The typical initial intravenous dosage of methylprednisolone is 500–1000 mg/d for 3–5 days.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES
items.question: Which medication may help manage emotional lability associated with glucocorticoid therapy?
items.explanation: Lithium carbonate (300 mg orally bid) may help manage emotional lability and insomnia associated with glucocorticoid therapy.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES

items.question: Which of the following agents is NOT mentioned as a first-line treatment for RMS?
items.explanation: Glatiramer acetate is less commonly used today for patients with new-onset MS and not typically a first-line treatment.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.question: Which factor is NOT considered when deciding to delay initiating RMS therapy?
options: low burden of disease by MRI
items.explanation: High attack frequency would not be a reason to delay treatment; normal exams, single attacks, and low MRI burden are considered.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.question: What is the recommended first-line therapy for JCV-negative RMS patients?
items.explanation: Natalizumab, a cell-trafficking inhibitor, is recommended for JCV-negative patients.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following agents has a high nuisance factor due to frequent injections?
items.explanation: Glatiramer acetate requires frequent injections, contributing to its high nuisance factor.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.question: Which disease-modifying therapy is approved for active secondary progressive multiple sclerosis (SPMS) despite not being specifically studied in this patient population?
items.explanation: Ocrelizumab is approved for active SPMS despite not having been specifically studied in this patient population.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.question: Which treatment option has shown to reduce the risk of progression in SPMS, particularly benefiting patients with a relapse in the 2 years prior and those with contrast-enhancing lesions on brain MRI?
items.explanation: Siponimod reduced the risk of progression in SPMS, especially for patients with recent relapses and contrast-enhancing lesions.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.question: Which off-label treatment option is considered largely ineffective for progressive multiple sclerosis?
options: CD19-targeted chimeric antigen receptor T cells
items.explanation: Autologous hematopoietic stem cell transplantation is largely ineffective for progressive MS.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.question: Which of the following therapies has been subjected to scientific scrutiny and found no benefit in improving disability in progressive forms of MS?
items.explanation: A double-blind trial found no benefit for high-dose biotin in progressive forms of MS.

items.question: Which therapy is NOT recommended for treating MS despite plausible involvement of infections with EBV or HHV 6?
items.explanation: Treatment with antiviral agents is not recommended for MS despite plausible involvement of infections.

items.question: Which proposed cause of MS was later refuted by multiple studies, leading to a recommendation against surgical intervention?
items.explanation: Multiple studies failed to confirm CCSVI as a cause of MS, leading to recommendations against surgical intervention.

items.question: Which medication is preferred over anticholinergic agents for managing detrusor hyperreflexia in MS patients due to fewer cognitive side effects?
options: Beta-3 adrenergic agonists such as mirabegron
items.explanation: Beta-3 adrenergic agonists are preferred over anticholinergic agents because they have fewer cognitive side effects.

items.question: What is the recommended initial management for detrusor hyperreflexia in MS patients?
options: Evening fluid restriction or frequent voluntary voiding
Immediate use of botulinum toxin injections
items.explanation: Initial management for detrusor hyperreflexia includes evening fluid restriction or frequent voluntary voiding.

items.question: Which of the following is a recommended dietary supplement due to its mild immunomodulatory effects in MS patients?
options: Long-chain fatty acids such as omega-3 oil tablets
items.explanation: Dietary supplementation with long-chain fatty acids like omega-3 oil tablets is recommended due to their mild immunomodulatory effects.

items.question: Which medication may be used for severe spasticity in MS patients when oral medications are insufficient?
options: Baclofen pump delivering medication directly into the CSF
items.explanation: A baclofen pump, which delivers medication directly into the CSF, is used for severe spasticity when oral medications are insufficient.

items.question: What percentage of RMS patients progressed to SPMS and required assistance with ambulation in older studies before disease-modifying therapies were available?
items.explanation: Between one-third and one-half of RMS patients progressed to SPMS and required assistance with ambulation.

items.question: What percentage of MS patients reached a level of disability requiring assistance with ambulation approximately 25 years after onset, according to older studies?
items.explanation: ~80% of MS patients reached this level of disability.

items.question: Which clinical feature at onset suggests a more favorable prognosis in MS?
options: More than two relapses in the first year
Appearance of motor signs during the first year
items.explanation: ON or sensory symptoms at onset suggest a more favorable prognosis.

items.subtopic: PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS
items.question: When considering the pregnancy year as a whole (i.e., 9 months of pregnancy plus 3 months postpartum), how is the overall disease course in pregnant MS patients affected?
options: The overall disease course is worsened.
The overall disease course is unaffected.
There are more attacks during gestation and fewer postpartum.
Disease-modifying therapy should be continued throughout pregnancy.
items.explanation: The passage states that the overall disease course is unaffected when considering the entire year of pregnancy plus the immediate postpartum period.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS

items.subtopic: PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS
items.question: What should be done with natalizumab and S1P drugs before conception in pregnant MS patients?
options: Replace them with an anti-CD20 B-cell therapy such as ocrelizumab.
Increase the dosage to prevent rebound disease activity.
items.explanation: The passage suggests replacing natalizumab and S1P drugs with an anti-CD20 B-cell therapy like ocrelizumab before conception to mitigate the risk of rebound disease activity.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS

items.subtopic: PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS
items.question: What is advised for patients receiving intravenously administered B-cell therapies who are contemplating pregnancy?
options: Stop the infusions 3–4 months prior to attempting to conceive.
Switch to oral medications during pregnancy.
Increase the frequency of infusions before conception.
items.explanation: The passage advises patients to stop receiving intravenously administered B-cell therapies 3–4 months prior to attempting to conceive.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS

items.statement: The past decade has seen no progress in understanding MS and developing therapies.
items.explanation: There has been tremendous progress in understanding MS mechanisms and developing effective therapies.

items.statement: MS affects approximately one million individuals worldwide.
items.explanation: One million individuals are affected by MS in the United States, with millions more worldwide.

items.statement: Uveitis is a common finding in patients with multiple sclerosis.
items.explanation: Uveitis is uncommon in MS and suggests alternative diagnoses.

items.statement: A sensory level below the horizontal line on the torso indicates a disturbance in the spinal cord.
items.explanation: Sensory impairment of the trunk and legs below this line suggests spinal cord involvement.

items.statement: Cognitive dysfunction in MS patients typically impairs activities of daily living.
items.explanation: Cognitive dysfunction sufficient to impair activities of daily living is rare in MS.

items.statement: Fatigue in MS patients can be exacerbated by elevated temperatures and depression.
items.explanation: Fatigue in MS can indeed be worsened by factors such as elevated temperatures and depression.

items.statement: All forms of MS involve both inflammation and neurodegeneration throughout the disease course.
items.explanation: The unitary view suggests that inflammation and neurodegeneration are present in most patients with MS throughout the disease.

items.statement: Primary progressive MS (PPMS) is characterized by acute attacks of neurologic dysfunction.
items.explanation: PPMS does not involve acute attacks; patients steadily decline in function from disease onset.

items.statement: Individuals who have never been infected with EBV have a very low risk of developing MS.
items.explanation: Individuals who have never been EBV infected (~5% of the population globally) have a very low MS risk, ~20-fold lower than in EBV-positive individuals.

items.statement: The prevalence of MS is higher in tropical regions compared to temperate zones.
items.explanation: The highest prevalence of MS is generally found in temperate zones, with a 10-fold to 20-fold lower prevalence in tropical regions.

items.statement: Familial aggregation of MS is primarily due to environmental factors.
items.explanation: Familial aggregation of MS is primarily due to genetic factors, although the environment also contributes.

items.statement: The variants identified for MS susceptibility are useful for diagnosis.
items.explanation: The variants lack specificity and sensitivity for MS, making them not useful for diagnosis.

items.statement: Specific antigenic targets in MS have been conclusively identified.
items.explanation: Specific antigenic targets in MS have never been conclusively identified.

items.statement: Activated astrocytes contribute directly to neuronal and myelin injury in MS.
items.explanation: Astrogliosis is a prominent feature of MS pathology, with activated astrocytes likely contributing directly to neuronal and myelin injury.

items.statement: Conduction slowing in demyelinated axons is due to reorganization of the axonal membrane for continuous propagation.
items.explanation: Conduction slowing occurs when demyelinated segments are reorganized to support slower, continuous nerve impulse propagation.

items.statement: A single diagnostic test can definitively diagnose Multiple Sclerosis.
items.explanation: There is no single diagnostic test for MS; diagnosis requires multiple criteria including clinical episodes and MRI findings.

items.statement: Lesions >6 mm located in the corpus callosum and periventricular white matter are diagnostically helpful for MS.
items.explanation: Lesions larger than 6 mm in these areas are particularly useful for diagnosing MS.

items.statement: The total volume of T2-weighted signal abnormality has a strong correlation with clinical disability.
items.explanation: There is only a weak correlation between the burden of disease on T2-weighted images and clinical disability.

items.statement: Oligoclonal bands (OCBs) are always present at the onset of multiple sclerosis (MS).
items.explanation: OCBs may be absent at the onset of MS.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID | EVOKED POTENTIALS

items.statement: Evoked potentials (EPs) are specific to multiple sclerosis (MS).
items.explanation: EP abnormalities are not specific to MS.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID | EVOKED POTENTIALS

items.statement: Patients with EDSS scores <3.5 are generally not disabled.
items.explanation: Most patients with EDSS scores <3.5 walk normally and are generally not disabled.

items.statement: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis (ADEM) is a consideration in hyperacute or postinfectious presentations of MS.
items.explanation: With hyperacute or postinfectious presentations, ADEM should be considered.

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES
items.statement: Mild attacks of multiple sclerosis are often treated with glucocorticoids.
items.explanation: Mild attacks are often not treated with glucocorticoids.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES

items.subtopic: ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES
items.statement: Plasma exchange may be beneficial for patients with fulminant demyelination unresponsive to glucocorticoids.
items.explanation: Plasma exchange can benefit patients with severe attacks not responding to glucocorticoid therapy.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · ACUTE ATTACKS OR INITIAL DEMYELINATING EPISODES

items.statement: Cross-trial comparisons of RMS therapies are considered accurate.
items.explanation: The passage states that cross-trial comparisons are inaccurate.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.statement: Vitamin D supplementation is recommended for all patients with RMS.
items.explanation: The passage advises correcting vitamin D deficiency in all MS patients.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.statement: Ocrelizumab has been shown to modify the course of primary progressive multiple sclerosis (PPMS).
items.explanation: Ocrelizumab is the only agent convincingly shown to modify the course of PPMS.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.subtopic: DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS
items.statement: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIg) is considered a first-line treatment for multiple sclerosis due to its low cost and well-established efficacy.
items.explanation: IVIg's use is limited by high cost, questions about optimal dose, and uncertainty about long-term disability impact.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 2) | OTHER OFF-LABEL TREATMENT OPTIONS

items.statement: Antibiotic treatment is recommended for managing MS.
items.explanation: Treatment with antibiotics is not recommended for MS.

items.statement: Patients should avoid unproven treatments that lack biological plausibility.
items.explanation: Patients are advised to avoid costly or potentially hazardous unproven treatments lacking biologic plausibility.

items.statement: Anticholinergic agents should be avoided in MS patients due to their potential to worsen cognitive dysfunction.
items.explanation: Anticholinergic side effects can worsen other MS symptoms, including cognitive dysfunction.

items.statement: Urinary tract infections in MS patients should be treated with prophylactic antibiotics as a first-line approach.
items.explanation: Prophylactic administration of antibiotics is sometimes necessary but may lead to colonization by resistant organisms.

items.statement: High-efficacy therapy has led to the elimination of relapses in MS.
items.explanation: Relapses are largely eliminated, but not completely.

items.statement: <10% of MS patients have a benign variant and never develop neurologic disability even when untreated.
items.explanation: It is estimated that <10% of MS patients have this benign variant.

items.subtopic: PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS
items.statement: Fertility treatments are associated with a significant increase in the risk of worsening MS disease.
items.explanation: The passage indicates that more recent studies show little if any additional risk from fertility treatments.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS

items.subtopic: PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS
items.statement: Marburg's variant of MS is characterized by remissions.
items.explanation: The passage states that Marburg's variant does not seem to have any remissions.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · PREGNANCY-RELATED ISSUES | CLINICAL VARIANTS OF MS

items.assertion: The clinical course of MS is extremely variable.
items.reason: MS can range from a relatively benign condition to a rapidly evolving and incapacitating disease.
items.explanation: The variability in the clinical course is due to the wide range of severity, from mild to severe forms.

items.assertion: A bilateral internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) is particularly suggestive of multiple sclerosis.
items.reason: Bilateral INO results from lesions in the medial longitudinal fasciculus, which are characteristic of MS.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.

items.assertion: Facial weakness in multiple sclerosis is typically associated with ipsilateral loss of taste sensation.
items.reason: This symptom pattern resembles idiopathic Bell's palsy.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; facial weakness in MS does not usually involve loss of taste, unlike Bell's palsy.

items.assertion: Lhermitte's symptom is a common feature of cervical spondylosis.
items.reason: Lhermitte's symptom can also occur with other disorders of the cervical spinal cord, such as cervical spondylosis.
items.explanation: Both Lhermitte's symptom and its occurrence in cervical spondylosis are true, but the reason does not explain why it is a common feature.

items.assertion: Trigeminal neuralgia is always related to MS.
items.reason: Most cases of trigeminal neuralgia are not MS-related; however, atypical features may suggest a possible link with MS.
items.explanation: The assertion that trigeminal neuralgia is always related to MS is false.

items.assertion: Progression independent of relapsing activity (PIRA) is the cause of disability accumulation in all forms of MS.
items.reason: PIRA events can occur insidiously, contributing to gradual worsening without clinical recognition.
items.explanation: Both PIRA as a cause and its silent occurrence are true and explain each other.

items.assertion: The risk of developing SPMS from RRMS is now estimated at <1% each year.
items.reason: This lower rate is likely due to the widespread use of increasingly effective therapies for MS.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the latter explaining the former.

items.reason: The strongest genetic susceptibility signal maps to the HLA-DRB1*1501 gene in the MHC class II region.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains that MS is an autoimmune disease due to its association with HLA genes.

items.assertion: MS-associated genetic variants have a large effect on individual risk.
items.reason: Whole-genome studies identified over 230 MS susceptibility variants.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because each variant has only a very small effect on MS risk, despite the identification of many variants.

items.assertion: B cells in MS patients produce antibodies that are specific to myelin antigens.
items.reason: Myelin-specific autoantibodies, some directed against an extracellular myelin protein, MOG, have been detected bound to degenerating myelin in MS plaques.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but many more antibodies appear to be directed against a diverse array of ubiquitous intracellular proteins seemingly unrelated to MS pathogenesis.

items.assertion: Microglial activation in progressive MS is triggered by macrophage and leukocyte infiltrates.
items.reason: Activated microglia are found in cortical plaques in the absence of macrophage and leukocyte infiltrates.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because microglial activation is thought to be triggered by proinflammatory B and T lymphocytes or tissue injury signals, not macrophages and leukocytes.

items.assertion: Clinical fluctuations in Multiple Sclerosis can vary from hour to hour.
items.reason: These fluctuations may be due to variable conduction block influenced by factors like raised body temperature or metabolic changes.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why clinical fluctuations occur.

items.assertion: Serial MRI studies in RRMS show that bursts of focal inflammatory disease activity occur frequently.
items.reason: Most disease activity is clinically silent early in MS.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the frequent occurrence of silent disease activity.

items.assertion: Evoked potential (EP) testing is most informative when the pathways studied are clinically uninvolved.
items.reason: An abnormal EP in a clinically uninvolved pathway can help diagnose MS even if other symptoms suggest different involvement.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · CEREBROSPINAL FLUID | EVOKED POTENTIALS

items.assertion: Anti-CD20 agents are preferred for RMS due to their high efficacy and safety.
items.reason: These agents provide outstanding control against relapsing disease and have a favorable safety profile.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.assertion: Older first-generation therapies like IFN-β or glatiramer acetate are commonly used for new-onset MS.
items.reason: These agents have a superb long-term track record for safety.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; these older therapies are less commonly used today for new-onset MS.
items.reference: Multiple Sclerosis · DISEASE-MODIFYING THERAPIES FOR MS (part 1)

items.assertion: Heat sensitivity in MS patients can be managed with cooling garments.
items.reason: Heat avoidance, air-conditioning, or cooling garments may help manage heat sensitivity.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.

items.assertion: Dalfampridine is used to treat spasticity in MS patients.
items.reason: Dalfampridine is an extended-release form of 4-aminopyridine, which improves weakness by blocking potassium channels.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because dalfampridine treats weakness, not spasticity; the reason is true.
